title: Walking the Numbered Registers
subtitle: "1p, then u and . to walk the cut-history backwards.
summary: After a deletion, "1p pastes the most recent cut. Press u then . — Vim undoes and pastes from the next-older numbered register. Walks all 9 cuts.

blocks.text: A delightful Vim quirk: the numbered registers (1-9) hold the last 9 things you cut. Combine with undo + dot, and you can browse your cut history one keypress at a time.

sequence.note: Repeat — but the dot bumps to register 2 next

blocks.title: Why . Bumps the Number
blocks.text:
When you do `"1p`, the dot register saves *that exact command* including the register name. Normally {key:.} would re-paste from `"1`. But Vim has a special case for the numbered-register paste: each time you {key:.} after a `"Np`, it **increments N** to the next numbered register.

This is implemented for exactly this use case — browsing the cut history. After {key:.} pastes from `"2`, the next {key:.} pastes from `"3`, and so on, until `"9`.

The undo before each dot rolls back the previous paste, so you're seeing one cut at a time, in order.
Use this when you've done several deletes and forgot which register has the text you want — just walk the chain.
